question: Define Natural Join and explain how it differs from Theta Join.
expectedPoints: Natural Join combines tuples with equal values for common attributes
Theta Join uses a general condition (θ) for combining tuples
Natural Join automatically removes duplicate join attributes
Theta Join explicitly specifies the condition

question: Explain the three types of Outer Joins with an example.
expectedPoints: Left Outer Join preserves all tuples from left relation
Right Outer Join preserves all tuples from right relation
Full Outer Join preserves tuples from both relations
Non-matching tuples get NULL values for missing attributes

question: What is the Division operation? When is it used? Give an example.
expectedPoints: Division R ÷ S returns tuples in X associated with every tuple in S
Used for 'for all' type queries
Example: Find students enrolled in ALL courses
Requires attributes of S to be subset of attributes of R

question: Explain Semijoin and state its advantage over full join.
expectedPoints: Semijoin returns tuples from one relation matching join condition
Only attributes from one relation appear in result
Reduces data transfer in distributed databases
Used in query optimization to reduce intermediate results

question: Express Intersection operation using basic relational algebra operations.
expectedPoints: R ∩ S = R - (R - S)
Or equivalently R ∩ S = S - (S - R)
Or R ∩ S = (R ∪ S) - (R - S) - (S - R)
Set difference is the fundamental operation used

question: What is a Self-join? When is it required? Explain with an example.
expectedPoints: Self-join joins a relation with itself
Requires rename operation to create tuple variables
Used to find relationships within same relation
Example: Find employee-supervisor pairs, or employees in same department

question: Differentiate between Equijoin and Natural Join.
expectedPoints: Equijoin uses only equality (=) as join condition
Natural Join is automatically based on equal common attributes
Equijoin may produce duplicate attribute columns
Natural Join removes duplicate join attribute column

question: Consider relations R(A,B) and S(B,C). Write relational algebra expressions for: (i) Natural Join R ⋈ S (ii) R Left Outer Join S (iii) R ÷ π_B(S) (iv) R Semijoin S
expectedPoints: (i) π_A,B,C(σ_R.B=S.B(R × S))
(ii) R ⋈ S ∪ (R - π_A,B(R ⋈ S)) × (NULL,NULL) or use outer join notation
(iii) π_A(R) - π_A((π_A(R) × S) - R) - uses set difference approach
(iv) R ⋉ S = π_R(R ⋈ S) or π_A,B(R ⋈ S)

question: Explain how you would find 'Students who have enrolled in ALL courses' using relational algebra division operation. Show step-by-step approach.
expectedPoints: Define relation Enrolled(StudentID, CourseID)
Define relation AllCourses(CourseID) with all courses
Use Division: Enrolled ÷ AllCourses
This returns StudentIDs associated with every CourseID in AllCourses
Alternative: π_StudentID(Enrolled) - π_StudentID((π_StudentID(Enrolled) × AllCourses) - Enrolled)

question: Prove that R ⋈θ S = σθ(R × S). Also explain how Natural Join can be derived using Selection, Projection, and Cartesian Product.
expectedPoints: Theta join first computes Cartesian product R × S
Then applies selection σ with condition θ
Natural join is equijoin on all common attributes
R ⋈ S = π_{all except duplicate}(σ_R.B1=S.B1 AND ... (R × S))
Projection removes duplicate join attribute column

question: Given relations Employee(EmpID, Name, DeptID) and Department(DeptID, DeptName), write RA expressions for: (a) Find employees in 'CS' department (b) Find departments with no employees (c) List all departments with their employees if any
expectedPoints: (a) σ_DeptName='CS'(Employee ⋈ Department) or π_Name(σ_DeptName='CS'(Employee ⋈ Department))
(b) π_DeptID(Department) - π_DeptID(Employee ⋈ Department)
(c) Employee ⟕ Department (Left Outer Join) or Department ⟕ Employee (Right Outer Join depending on requirement)
